role: non-Anthropic decorrelation vote on the cross-linguistic-cc-replication-scope decision (PROTOCOL §2)

verdict.Q1: A (German) — best preserves methodological credibility: strong panel competence, easier source-auditing, fewer item-translation failure modes matter more than maximizing typological distance; Japanese is a better next lever, not the first.
verdict.Q2: B (add a corpus-frequency-matched control) — same-word controls are not enough for a cross-linguistic anti-template argument; a model could still exploit residual frequency/co-occurrence regularities in the target language; a matched control materially strengthens the inference that the effect is constructional, not just distributional.
verdict.Q3: A (internal-contrast-only, no human claim) — blocking on a non-English human anchor is too strong a gate for an already well-posed behavioral question.
verdict.freeze_condition: Require a pre-frozen bilingual item audit by a competent German speaker plus a documented frequency-matching recipe for the new control set before any model runs.

how_weighed: Convergent with the fresh reviewer on Q1 (German now) and Q3 (internal-contrast-only). DIVERGENT on Q2 (vote B vs reviewer's provisional A): the fresh reviewer weighed this + the s208/s210 C4-confound lesson and MOVED its verdict to Q2-B (additive). The vote's 'competent German speaker audit' freeze condition is charter-incompatible (HARD no-human-subjects rule) and was REPLACED by the reviewer with a documented lead-agent self-audit against ingested German CC grammar sources (condition viii).
